summary: This post is a transcript of a recorded discussion, so it doesn't argue a single main claim — open the original to read the conversation.

The main claim is: Unfortunately, interpreting the insides of neural networks is known to be very difficult, so the question becomes: what is the minimal set of properties a network must have in order for adding activation additions to work?
I make some progress on this question by gathering data on whether or not activation additions work on an old 3 layer, fully connected MNIST network I had laying around.

The main claim is: Had a stray thought that I think is worth exploring: perhaps disagreements over how to respond to AI risks are heavily influenced by personal biases in how to assess risk in general such that very different conclusions can be drawn from ev…

top_comment.text: I would say that it should be done using google forms? For usability of large statistics.

summary: The main claim is: We think that, for systems more capable than Claude and GPT-4, we are now at the point where we need to check carefully that new models do not have sufficient capabilities to replicate autonomously or cause catastrophic harm – it’s no long…
But models are able to succeed at several of the necessary components.
The main claim is: Assume you had a fully human-understandable system, and you could understand its current capabilities.
Conjecture's post is concise, which means it leaves out many details.

top_comment.author: Connor Leahy
top_comment.text: Thanks for this! These are great questions! We have been collecting questions from the community and plan to write a follow up post addressing them in the next couple of weeks.
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: We have been collecting questions from the community and plan to write a follow up post addressing them in the next couple of weeks.

summary: The main claim is: Let's start with our threat model for alignment -- how we expect AGI development to go and the main sources of risk.
I would say that Scalable Alignment is mostly working on components and the other two teams are mostly working on enablers.

top_comment.author: Daniel Kokotajlo
top_comment.text:
I'm so glad you are making a plan and sharing it publicly! 

Fun, possibly impactful idea: Have a livestreamed chat with Jan Leike (or some other representative from OpenAI's alignment team) where you discuss and critique each other's plans & discuss how you can support each other by sharing research etc.
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: I'm so glad you are making a plan and sharing it publicly!

summary: The main claim is: If we want more people in the world who understand AGI safety, we need a place where it's accepted and encouraged to ask about the basics.
This is a safe space for people to ask stupid questions, so be kind!

The main claim is: The main difference is that they use transitivity +"recklessness" to get a contradiction whereas I argue directly from "non-timidity." I also end up violating a dominance principle which seems even more surprising to violate, but at this p…
It seems like they face an infinite mixture of possible outcomes.
The main claim is: Therefore, as long as we believe the demonstrator is safe, the agent should be as well.
It turns out that neural networks can express polynomials quite easily.
The main claim is: If the agent fails on one of these desiderata, it is definitely not corrigible.
One desideratum for corrigibility is then that the agent must never attempt to prevent its shutdown button from being pressed.
The main claim is: When there's only one human, we must grapple with the problem of what to align our AI system to.
